Class StartYourMonkeys

java.lang.Object
com.github.mrgarbagegamer.StartYourMonkeys
public class StartYourMonkeys extends Object
The main application entry point and orchestrator for the Lights Out puzzle solver.

This class is responsible for initializing and coordinating the entire puzzle-solving process. It sets up a producer-consumer architecture where generators act as producers, generating potential solutions, and "monkeys" act as consumers, validating them.

Architecture Role

The main(String[]) method serves as the central hub, performing the following key tasks:

  • Parses command-line arguments for numClicks, numThreads, and puzzleNumber.
  • Instantiates the correct Grid implementation (e.g., Grid35) for the selected puzzle.
  • Initializes the CombinationQueueArray, the communication backbone for distributing work.
  • Configures and starts a ForkJoinPool for the recursive generators.
  • Launches a pool of monkeys.
  • Manages the application lifecycle, from submitting the initial root task to gracefully shutting down and reporting results.

Performance and Threading

The orchestration logic within this class has a minimal performance footprint (effectively O(1)), as the computational heavy lifting is delegated to the generator and worker threads. The main thread's primary role is setup, coordination, and shutdown.

Orchestration is single-threaded. The main thread blocks after submitting the root generation task and subsequently joins all worker threads, ensuring a clean shutdown and accurate result reporting. This makes it the ideal location for initializing static, shared resources before the concurrent phase begins.

Since:
2025.04 - Multi-threaded Refactor
Performance Characteristics:
~O(1) for orchestration tasks.
Threading Model:
Thread-safe; single-threaded orchestration.
Memory Management:
Pre-allocation of shared resources to minimize runtime overhead.

  • Field Details

    • logger

      private static final Logger logger
      The primary logger for the application, configured for high-performance, asynchronous logging.

      In a highly concurrent application, standard synchronous logging (like System.out.println) can become a major bottleneck. To avoid this, we use Log4j2 with an asynchronous configuration. This allows application threads to offload log messages to a background thread with minimal blocking, preventing logging from impacting the performance of the core solving algorithm.

      Since:
      2025.05 - Log4j2 Integration
      See Also:
      • CombinationMessage
      • Logger
      • Logger.info(String)
      • Logger.info(String, Object)
      • Logger.info(String, Object, Object)
      • LogManager
      • LogManager.getLogger()
      • LogManager.shutdown()
      • Log4j2 Asynchronous Logging
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) for logging operations.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; designed for concurrent use.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed overhead for asynchronous logging buffers.

    • DEFAULT_NUM_CLICKS

      private static final int DEFAULT_NUM_CLICKS
      The default number of clicks to test for a solution.

      Based on prior analysis, no solution with 16 or fewer clicks exists for the primary target, Q35. This default is set to the next logical step in the brute-force search.

      Since:
      2025.08 - Enhanced Documentation of Codebase
      See Also:
      • main(String[])
      • Constant Field Values
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access time.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe as a static final constant.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

    • DEFAULT_NUM_THREADS

      private static final int DEFAULT_NUM_THREADS
      The default number of threads to use for both generators and workers.

      This value is tuned for a high-core-count development machine (16+ cores). The application allocates half of these threads to the ForkJoinPool for the generators and the other half to the monkeys.

      Since:
      2025.08 - Enhanced Documentation of Codebase
      See Also:
      • main(String[])
      • Constant Field Values
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) access time.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe as a static final constant.
      Memory Management:
      Fixed memory footprint of 4 bytes as a primitive int.

      public StartYourMonkeys()

  • Method Details

    • main

      public static void main(String[] args)
      The main entry point for the solver application.

      This method orchestrates the entire solving process, from initialization to shutdown. It follows a structured sequence:

      1. Argument Parsing: Reads numClicks, numThreads, and puzzleNumber from command-line arguments, with sane defaults.
      2. Component Initialization: Selects the appropriate Grid subclass and initializes the CombinationQueueArray and other shared resources.
      3. Monkey Creation: Spawns a pool of TestClickCombination threads that immediately begin waiting for work.
      4. Generator Execution: Creates a ForkJoinPool and submits a root CombinationGeneratorTask. The main thread blocks using invoke(CombinationGeneratorTask), waiting for the entire generation process (including all forked subtasks) to complete or for a solution to be found.
      5. Graceful Shutdown: Once generation finishes, it flushes any remaining work from generator-local batches, signals to the workers that no more work is coming, and waits for them to terminate using Thread.join().
      6. Result Reporting: Reports the outcome (solution found or not found), verifies the solution if one exists, and logs the total elapsed time before shutting down the logger.

      ForkJoinPool Behavior

      A key decision was to use invoke() to block the main thread. Early prototypes using non-blocking approaches with ForkJoinPool.awaitQuiescence(long, java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit) proved unreliable, as awaitQuiescence did not consistently wait for dynamically forked subtasks to complete. The current blocking approach provides a robust and predictable mechanism for managing the generator lifecycle.

      Parameters:
      args - Command-line arguments:
      • args[0]: Number of clicks to test (e.g., 17).
      • args[1]: Total number of threads to use (e.g., 16).
      • args[2]: The puzzle ID to solve (13, 22, or 35).
      Throws:
      IllegalArgumentException - if any command-line arguments are invalid.
      Since:
      2025.04 - Multi-threaded Refactor
      Performance Characteristics:
      ~O(1) for most orchestration tasks.
      Threading Model:
      Single-threaded for most of orchestration, coordinating multiple threads.
      Memory Management:
      Pre-allocates several shared resources to minimize runtime overhead.

    • formatElapsedTime

      private static String formatElapsedTime(long millis)
      Formats a millisecond duration into a human-readable "Elapsed time: Xh Ym Zs Wms" string.
      Parameters:
      millis - The elapsed time in milliseconds.
      Returns:
      A formatted String representing the duration.
      Since:
      2025.06 - Millisecond Precision to Elapsed Time Formatting
      See Also:
      • System.currentTimeMillis()
      • StringBuilder
      • StringBuilder.toString()
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(1) operations and string formatting.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; does not modify shared state.
      Memory Management:
      Allocates a small, fixed-size StringBuilder for formatting and returns a new String.

    • logGrid

      private static void logGrid(Grid grid)
      Logs the Grid's string representation line-by-line to avoid logging issues.
      Parameters:
      grid - The Grid to log.
      Since:
      2025.11 - Grid Logging Utility Introduction
      Performance Characteristics:
      O(NUM_ROWS) for splitting and logging each line.
      Threading Model:
      Thread-safe; does not modify the Grid.
      Memory Management:
      Allocates a temporary array of String lines for logging.

Performance-optimized brute-force solver for hexagonal grid Lights Out puzzles
